coredata.dc:title: The molecular mechanisms of silica nanomaterials enhancing the rice (Oryza sativa L.) resistance to planthoppers (Nilaparvata lugens Stal)

coredata.dc:description: Herein, fluorescent silica (F-SiO2) ENMs (50 nm) were synthesized, which could be taken up and translocated from rice root to shoot, promoting the plant growth and resistance to planthopper compared with Si ion fertilizers under hydroponic conditions. Particularly, upon exposure F-SiO2 ENMs (5 mg‧L−1) suspension for 9 days, the fresh and dry weight (FW and DW) of shoot, the root length, surface area, and tip number were increased by 33.58%, 65.22%, 15.26%, 20.26% and 29.01%, respectively. Notably, in the presence of planthopper, the shoot FW and DW still increased by 61.88% and 114.75%, respectively. The increased lignin content (by 30.13%) and formation of silica cells in stem after F-SiO2 ENMs exposure (5 mg‧L−1) could be mechanical barriers against planthoppers. The transcriptome data revealed that F-SiO2 ENMs could upregulate the expression of genes involved in plant-pathogen interactions, plant hormone signal transduction, glucose metabolism and carbon fixation pathway, promoting the growth and resistance of rice seedlings. Our findings provide first evidence for the underlying molecular mechanisms of SiO2 ENMs enhancing the rice resistance to planthopper.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characteristic of F-SiO2 ENMs. a, schematic diagram of F-SiO2 ENMs synthesis; b, fluorescence spectra of CDs and F-SiO2 ENMs (excitation wavelength at 360 nm); c, TEM image of F-SiO2 ENMs.

Rice growth promoted by F-SiO2 ENMs, SiO2 ENMs and Si ion fertilizers. a, Growth of rice roots (root length, surface area, average diameter, and root tips) in the presence of F-SiO2 ENMs, SiO2 ENMs and Si ion fertilizers; b, FW (b) and DW (c) of rice shoot and root after F-SiO2 ENMs, SiO2 ENMs and Si ion fertilizers exposure. Different superscript letters indicate significant differences among treatments (p < 0.05, n = 5).
CLSM images of rice seedlings (leaf, stem, and root) upon F-SiO2 ENMs (5 mg‧L−1) exposure.

The pest resistance and lignin formation in rice stimulated by F-SiO2, SiO2 ENMs and Si ion fertilizers. a, Phenotypic image of different treatment groups; FW (b) and DW (c) of rice after planthopper infestation; d, lignin content in rice of different treatments; e, fluorescence microscope images of lignin in rice stems, and the corresponding heat map. (p < 0.05, n = 5).

The molecular mechanisms of pest resistance in rice enhanced by F-SiO2 ENMs. a, Venn diagram shows the numbers of differentially expressed genes; b, the number of differentially expressed genes; c, gene expression pattern of rice seedlings with or without F-SiO2 ENMs treatment. Blue and red colors represent the down- and up-regulation, respectively; d, the scheme of molecular mechanisms of rice resistance to planthopper improved by F-SiO2 ENMs. (q < 0.05, n = 3). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: The molecular mechanisms of silica nanomaterials enhancing the rice (Oryza sativa L.) resistance to planthoppers (Nilaparvata lugens Stal)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, fluorescent silica (F-SiO2) ENMs (50 nm) were synthesized, which could be taken up and translocated from rice root to shoot, promoting the plant growth and resistance to planthopper compared with Si ion fertilizers under hydroponic conditions. Particularly, upon exposure F-SiO2 ENMs (5 mg‧L−1) suspension for 9 days, the fresh and dry weight (FW and DW) of shoot, the root length, surface area, and tip number were increased by 33.58%, 65.22%, 15.26%, 20.26% and 29.01%, respectively. Notably, in the presence of planthopper, the shoot FW and DW still increased by 61.88% and 114.75%, respectively. The increased lignin content (by 30.13%) and formation of silica cells in stem after F-SiO2 ENMs exposure (5 mg‧L−1) could be mechanical barriers against planthoppers. The transcriptome data revealed that F-SiO2 ENMs could upregulate the expression of genes involved in plant-pathogen interactions, plant hormone signal transduction, glucose metabolism and carbon fixation pathway, promoting the growth and resistance of rice seedlings. Our findings provide first evidence for the underlying molecular mechanisms of SiO2 ENMs enhancing the rice resistance to planthopper.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing global population requires a substantial increase of crop yields to meet the estimated increase in food demand of 50–80% by 2050 (Lowry et al., 2019). However, over 59,000 pests and diseases threaten agricultural production globally, causing an estimated 27% loss of crop yields (Zhang, 2018). Meanwhile, about 4 million tons of pesticides are used each year, and no more than 10% of pesticides could be utilized by plants (Gilbertson et al., 2020; Zhang, 2018). The pesticides remained in the environment could harm organisms and cause serious environmental issues (Klarich et al., 2017). Therefore, it is urgent to find sustainable agricultural anti-pest practices.

Over the past decade, the application of engineered nanomaterials (ENMs) in agriculture has gained extensive attention (Lowry et al., 2019; Kah et al., 2019). Several studies have demonstrated that ENMs can increase crop yields, improve nutritional quality, and strengthen plant resistance (Lowry et al., 2019; Kah et al., 2019). Particularly, ENMs can be used as nanopesticides to protect plant against pests, improving pesticide efficacy and reducing pesticide residues and environmental pollution (Adisa et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2019). Nano-structure alumina (Al2O3) was equally efficient as, or even more powerful than the commercial one on the suppression of Sitophilus oryzae (L.) and Rhyzopertha dominica (F.) at varying ambient humidity levels (Stadler et al., 2012). Although these metal or metal oxide ENMs can prevent pest infestation, the improper or excessive use in agriculture can still damage ecosystems and create substantial problems (Zhang et al., 2020). Currently, emerging research has been focusing on the development of environmentally friendly ENMs for pest control.

Silicon (Si) is the second most abundant element in the earth's crust (Landré et al., 2020). Although being a nonessential element for plant growth and development (Gaur et al., 2020), growing evidence reveals that Si plays an important role in enhancing plant resistance to pests through forming physical barriers, regulating plant hormone signals (jasmonic acid (JA), salicylic acid (SA) and ethylene (ET)), and inducing emissions of herbivore-induced plant volatiles (HIPVs) (Islam et al., 2020). Generally, the bioavailabilities of conventional Si-based fertilizers are low (Li and Delvaux, 2019; Wang et al., 2015), whereas SiO2 ENMs are more efficient due to their high biocompatibility, large surface area, tunable pore size for high loading capacity, and ability of targeted and controlled release by surface functionalization and polymer coatings (Cheng et al., 2020). For instance, the insecticide imidacloprid was effectively loaded onto mesoporous SiO2 ENMs with different pore sizes, morphologies and mesoporous structures for termite control (Jeelani et al., 2020). Prado et al. reported that SiO2 ENMs was modified with carboxylic acid for the controlled release of 2,4-D and picloram (Prado et al., 2011). Moreover, soil application of 5% SiO2 ENMs can significantly reduce the harm of oriental armyworm (reduction by 37.16%) (Mousa et al., 2014). To date, most attention has been denoted to addressing the roles of SiO2 ENMs as carriers and their property of slow controlled release of agrochemicals, whereas the potential of SiO2 ENMs themselves to regulate plant resistance to pests and the underlying mechanisms remain to be explored.

Rice (Oryza sativa L.) as the major source of food for human, feeds over half the world's population. However, the global rice production is seriously threatened by planthoppers, destroying as much as 20 million hectares of rice each year in China (Hu et al., 2019). The applications of traditional pesticides to control planthoppers leads to ecological damage, public health problem, and high resistance of planthoppers to most insecticides (Wu et al., 2018a; Xu et al., 2017). Therefore, the development of sustainable prevention and control technology of planthoppers is urgently needed. Improving the host resistance is a green, economical and efficient strategy to prevent planthopper infestation (Horgan, 2018). Although more than 30 planthopper resistance genes have been genetically identified, and at least 8 of them have been cloned, planthoppers can still adapt to the modifications rapidly (Pan et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2016). It is found that carbon ENMs can enter the cell of rice, interact with DNA, and increase the relative expression of Os06g32600, leading to an enhanced resistance to disease (Li et al., 2018a). Moreover, core genes of maize for lignin synthesis were up-regulated after upon La2O3 ENMs (Yue et al., 2018). So far, whether SiO2 ENMs can activate the gene expression of plant resistance to pests remain to be studied.

Si is a component of the cell wall of rice, increasing the hardness and abrasiveness of plant tissues, consequently impacting oral parts of phytophagous insects and reducing food intake (Leroy et al., 2019). Therefore, we hypothesized that SiO2 ENMs could promote the expressions of genes related to pest control and lignin formation, enhancing the resistance of rice to planthopper. The following three aspects were specifically explored: (1) the growth promoting effects and internalization of SiO2 ENMs in rice seedlings; (2) the planthopper resistance of rice seedlings enhanced by SiO2 ENMs; and (3) changes in the transcription processes of rice seedlings in the presence of SiO2 ENMs. This is the first evidence revealing the mechanisms of SiO2 ENMs-enhanced planthopper resistance of rice seedlings, which will support the application of nanotechnology in sustainable pest management.

Citric acid (CA, AR, >99%), anhydrous ethanol (C2H6O), ammonia (NH3‧H2O, AR), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, AR, 98%), nitric acid (HNO3, GR, >99%), glacial acetic acid (C2H4O2, AR, 99.5%), formaldehyde (HCHO, AR, 37–40%), xylene (C8H10, AR, >99%), acetyl bromide (C2H3BrO, AR, 25%), hydroxylamine hydrochloride (HONH2‧HCl, AR, 98.5%), hydrofluoric acid (HF, AR, >99%), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR, >99%) were purchased from Sinopsin group chemical reagent., LTD. All reagents were used without further purification.

The carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by dissolving 4 g CA in 40 mL deionized water and heating at 180 °C for 2 h. The CDs were collected by freeze drying. The fluorescent SiO2 (F-SiO2) ENMs were prepared as follows. In brief, C2H6O (125 mL), prepared CDs (100 mg), and NH3‧H2O (6.25 mL) were mixed under stirring (420 rpm). Then, TEOS (0.25 mL) was quickly added. Under stirring for 1 h, the mixture of TEOS (4 mL) and C2H6O (10 mL) were added dropwise. After stirring for 24 h, the F-SiO2 ENMs were collected by centrifuging at 5000 rpm for 10 min, and placed in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 6 h. The size and shape distribution of F-SiO2 ENMs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM–2100, Nippon electronics co, JPN). Fluorescence spectra were obtained from an F–7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, JPN).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plant growth, NPs exposure and pest test
ce:section.ce:para: Rice seeds (Y Liangyou 2) were obtained from China National Hybrid Rice R&D Center/Hunan Hybrid Rice Research Center. The seeds were sterilized in 5% sodium hypochlorite solution for 10 min and were then rinsed with deionized water for three times. After immersing in deionized water for 8 h, the seeds were germinated on wet filter paper in a petri dish until the rice plant height reached about 5 cm. Then uniform seedlings were selected and transferred into pots (1.5 L each) and acclimated hydroponically in 25% strength Hoagland nutrient solution for two weeks. The 25% strength nutrient solution was renewed every three days. After two weeks, the seedlings were transferred to deionized water spiked with different concentrations of SiO2 ENMs (1 and 5 mg‧L−1), F-SiO2 ENMs (1, 5 and 10 mg‧L−1), and Si ion fertilizers (Na2SiO3, 1 and 5 mg‧L−1), the exposure suspensions were renewed every three days. Three replicates were established for each treatment with six parallels. Plant roots and shoots were sampled after 9 days treatment, the fresh weight (FW), dry weight (DW) of rice plants as well as lignin content of rice stem and the morphology parameters of rice root were recorded. The translocation of F-SiO2 ENMs was observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, A1, Nikon, JPN) according to a previous method (Wang et al., 2018).

For the test of resistance to planthopper, uniform rice seedlings after exposure to F-SiO2 ENMs (0, 5 mg‧L−1) for 9 days were infested to planthopper. Briefly, rice seedlings were grown in ceramic pots covered with light-transmitting mesh, under which planthopper nymphs of second to third instar were released (10 planthoppers in each pot) (Fig. S9b). After one week, the planthoppers were removed from plants, the rice roots and shoots were sampled for further investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of Si content in rice stems and roots
ce:section.ce:para: The contents of Si element in rice roots and stems were measured by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP–MS, iCAP–TQ, Thermo Fisher, Germany). Briefly, 25 mg dried rice sample was digested (200 °C, 1600 W, 30 min) in a microwave accelerated reaction system (CEM corp, Matthews. NC) with a mixture of guaranteed reagent HNO3, ultrapure water (4:3) and two drops of hydrofluoric acid. The digested solution was filtered with a microporous membrane (0.22 μm) and diluted to 50 mL for the ICP–MS analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of lignin content in rice stems
ce:section.ce:para: The lignin content of rice shoot was determined according to a previous method (Fukuda and Komamine, 1982). The fluorescent intensity of lignin in rice stem was observed by a fluorescence microscope under 405 nm using a digital camera, samples were prepared using the paraffin method as described by Wu et al and Deng et al (Deng et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2018b).

The planthopper-infested stems pre-exposed with (5 mg‧L−1) or without F-SiO2 ENMs were sampled, frozen with liquid nitrogen and preserved under −80 °C. All samples were subsequently submitted to Novogene Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China), for RNA sequencing. For each gene, expression level was measured by Fragments Per Kilobases Per Millionreads (FPKM). Besides, differences in RNA expression were analyzed by DESeq2 (padj≤0.05, |log2FoldChange| > 0). Gene function annotation was conducted by comparison to the KEGG and GO databases (Figs. S10–S11).

All treatments were conducted at least in triplicate. Data were presented as a mean ± standard deviation. One-way ANOVA with the Fisher LSD test was used to analyze the significant difference among different treatments using Origin Statistics 2019b at p < 0.05.

The preparation process of F-SiO2 ENMs involved the addition of pre-synthesized CDs (blue fluorescence, 2–4 nm, Fig. S1a) into the Stӧber process, followed by the coating onto the solid matrix of SiO2 (Fig. 1a). The F-SiO2 ENMs showed strong blue fluorescence at 440 nm under 360 nm excitation wavelength, which corresponded to the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. 1b). Moreover, the blue fluorescence could be observed using a portable UV lamp (inset of Fig. 1b). The F-SiO2 ENMs possessed spherical morphology with an average size at 50 nm (Fig. 1c). The evenly located CDs could be clearly seen on the SiO2 shell, which had a homogeneous size of 2–4 nm (Fig. 1a and Fig. S1b). These results confirmed that the fluorescent and spherical F-SiO2 ENMs were synthesized successfully. Moreover, prepared F-SiO2 ENMs showed high time, photo, ions and pH-stability (Fig. S2), and the CDs could not be released from the solid matrix of SiO2 (Fig. S3). Since the fluorescent CDs were coated onto the solid matrix of SiO2 during the Stӧber process, which were hard to be released in the SiO2 shell (Cui et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rice growth promoted by SiO2 and F-SiO2 ENMs
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, a promoting effect was observed. The plant height was increased significantly after exposure to SiO2 or F-SiO2 ENMs compared to control and Si ion fertilizers at same dose (Fig. S4a). Notably, the height under 5 mg‧L−1 F-SiO2 ENMs exposure increased by 20.05%. Previous studies demonstrated that ENMs can enhance plant growth (Lowry et al., 2019). For instance, the growth of wheat root can be improved (10-fold) by CDs at 150 mg‧L−1 (Tripathi and Sarkar, 2015). The tomato seed germination, seed vigor index, seedling FW, and DW can be significantly increased by SiO2 ENMs (Siddiqui and Al-Whaibi, 2014). Generally, the root growth under ENMs exposure was better than those in the control and Si ion fertilizers (Fig. S4b). Longer length, larger surface area, and more tips were observed for roots in the ENMs treated groups, and F-SiO2 ENMs at 5 mg‧L−1 led to an increase of these parameters by 15.26%, 20.26% and 29.01%, respectively (Fig. 2a). Root is an essential part of plant, which mainly absorbs water and nutrients for plant growth and development. It is indicated that ENMs are beneficial to plant root (Li et al., 2016). For example, ZnO ENMs (10 mg‧kg−1) significantly increased the root length (66.3%) and area (73.5%) of cluster bean (Raliya and Tarafdar, 2013). Our results indicated that SiO2 ENMs have stimulated the growth of rice root.

It is reported that SiO2 ENMs (1.5–7.5 g‧L−1) or CDs (30 mg‧L−1) can increase the biomass of Cucurbita pepo L (Siddiqui et al., 2014) or lettuce (Zheng et al., 2017). In this study, the biomass of rice seedlings was increased significantly after SiO2 ENMs and F-SiO2 ENMs exposure (Fig. 2b and c). No significant difference was observed for plants exposed to 5 and 10 mg‧L−1 F-SiO2 ENMs, but both outperformed those treated with 1 mg‧L−1 F-SiO2 ENMs, indicating that 5 mg‧L−1 could be the optimal concentration considering the intention to minimize the introduction of ENMs into the environment. Under 5 mg‧L−1 F-SiO2 ENMs, the shoot FW and DW were increased by 33.58% and 41.69%, and the root FW and DW were increased by 65.22% and 29.92%, respectively. Although the FW of root exposed to 5 mg‧L−1 F-SiO2 ENMs was higher than that of SiO2 ENMs, no obvious difference was observed for the DW. The difference in FW of rice root from F-SiO2 ENMs and SiO2 ENMs treatments may be attributed to the root vigor and water uptake promoted by the CDs (Wang et al., 2018). Our results indicated that F-SiO2 and SiO2 ENMs had a beneficial effect on rice seedlings growth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uptake and translocation of F-SiO2 ENMs in rice
ce:section.ce:para: Si content was measured in the rice seedlings after 9 d exposure to F-SiO2 ENMs. Both 1 mg‧L−1 SiO2 ENMs and 10 mg‧L−1 F-SiO2 ENMs led a significant increase in Si content of rice root, while no increase or even lower Si content was observed in root exposed to 5 mg‧L−1 SiO2 ENMs, F-SiO2 ENMs (1 and 5 mg‧L−1), and Si ion fertilizers (1 and 5 mg‧L−1) (Fig. S5b). The Si content in stem showed opposite results. 10 mg‧L−1 F-SiO2 ENMs and 1 mg‧L−1 SiO2 ENMs did not increase or lowered the Si content in stem, whereas significant increase (13.96%, 15.80% and 11.77%, respectively) was detected upon SiO2 ENMs (5 mg‧L−1) and F-SiO2 ENMs (1 and 5 mg‧L−1) exposure (Fig. S5a). ENMs can be absorbed by the roots and transported to other tissues of plants through xylem (Wang et al., 2012). In this study, the increased Si content in rice stem demonstrated that Si was taken up by the root and transported upwards. However, the transport efficiency of Si or SiO2 ENMs under 1 mg‧L−1 (SiO2 ENMs) and 10 mg‧L−1 (F-SiO2 ENMs) might be low, resulting in the ENMs accumulation in the rice root (Fig. S5b). However, the mechanism of different Si accumulation and transport patterns in rice at high or low concentration of F-SiO2 and SiO2 ENMs exposure in this study is not clear yet.

ENMs are known to be taken up, translocated, and transformed in plants (Zhao et al., 2017), whereas the detection of ENMs in situ remains to be one of the big challenges. In recent years, fluorescent ENMs application in agriculture production has gained much attention due to their advantages in light conversion and direct in situ observation by CLSM (Chen et al., 2018; Li et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2018). To further demonstrate the transport of SiO2 ENMs from roots to shoots, CDs were applied as a fluorescent indicator and combined with SiO2 ENMs. As shown in Fig. 3 , the root, stem, and leave exhibited clear blue fluorescence under exposure to F-SiO2 ENMs (5 mg‧L−1), while the control treatment had no fluorescent signal (Fig. S6). Particularly, the strongest blue fluorescence appeared in the rice stems (Fig. 3). Therefore, SiO2 ENMs could be taken up and translocated by rice from root to shoot after root exposure. Taken together, SiO2 ENMs could promote the growth of plants and the fluorescence coating enabled the in situ observation of plant uptake and transfer of ENMs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pest resistance and lignin formation in rice enhanced by SiO2 ENMs
ce:section.ce:para: Knowing that SiO2 ENMs can be transported from roots to shoots, we further explored whether SiO2 ENMs could enhance the planthopper resistance of rice. After planthopper infestation, the growth of rice without pre-treatment of F-SiO2 ENMs was obviously inhibited by planthopper (Fig. S9b), and the leaves gradually changed from green to yellow (Fig. 4a). However, pre-treatment with SiO2 ENMs (5 mg‧L−1) and F-SiO2 ENMs (5 mg‧L−1) maintained the health of leaves and even enhanced the growth of rice after planthopper infestation compared to the control and Si ion fertilizers (Fig. 4a). The FW of rice shoots exposed to 5 mg‧L−1 SiO2 and F-SiO2 ENMs were increased by 27.75% and 61.88%, and the corresponding DW were increased by 49.83% and 114.75%, respectively (Fig. 4b and c). Moreover, the growth of rice root under ENMs exposure was better than that in the control and Si ion fertilizers (Fig. S8). The FW of rice roots exposed to 5 mg‧L−1 SiO2 and F-SiO2 ENMs were increased by 26.67% and 27.28%, and the corresponding DW were increased by 36.58% and 91.56%, respectively (Fig. 4b and c). Thus, pre-exposure to ENMs prevented the damage caused by the planthopper, indicating an enhanced pest resistance of rice to planthopper by SiO2 ENMs (5 mg‧L−1) and F-SiO2 ENMs (5 mg‧L−1). Fig. 4b and c showed that F-SiO2 ENM was better than SiO2 ENM in strengthening the pest resistance, the reason could be the enhanced photosynthesis and water uptake of rice seedlings in the presence of CDs coating (Li et al., 2018b; Park et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2018). A considerable amount of research demonstrated that ENMs can be potential pesticides to protect crop production. Ag ENMs (100 mg‧kg−1) significantly inhibited plant pathogenic fungi (Kim et al., 2012). Cu ENMs (0.2 mg‧L−1) could suppress the growth of bacterial blight on pomegranate (Mondal and Mani, 2012). However, exposure to these metal-based ENMs (Ag, Cu) could lead to the bio-accumulation in the food chain, threatening the health of ecosystem and even human health (Petersen et al., 2019). Si is known to be beneficial on eliminating biotic and abiotic stresses of plants (Zhao et al., 2020), moreover, to our best of knowledge, no potential risk of SiO2 ENMs entering the food chain has been reported so far.

The preventions of pest and disease hold great significance for agricultural production. Although agrochemicals are applied to improve agriculture production, the health problems and environmental risks caused by pesticide residuals are widely concerned by the public. It has been suggested that nano-agrochemicals may be superior to conventional products. For instance, Kumar et al. demonstrated that encapsulated (pesticide-loaded nanoformulation) formulation is more effective and had lower toxicity than even plane pesticide (Kumar et al., 2014). The LC50 of nano-permethrin to Culex quinquefasciatus was 0.117 mg‧L−1, and the efficacy was increased by 83.64% compared to bulk permethrin (Anjali et al., 2010). Saini et al. showed that nano-formulation of pyridalyl is 2.26 and 6.25 times more effective against Helicoverpa armigera than the technical product and commercial formulation, respectively (Saini et al., 2014).

Si is a component of plant cell wall, maintaining the thickness and stability of cell wall (Hussain et al., 2020). The silica cell analogs were discovered by CLSM and optical microscope as shown in the bright field image of rice stem (Figs. 3 and S7). Silica cells were rectangular epidermal cells, which filled with silicoid in the epidermis of stems and leaves (Kaufman et al., 1985; Kumar et al., 2017). Previous studies reported that Si treatment could strengthen the microstructures, and form silica cells as a mechanical barrier in rice against stem borers and planthoppers (Dorairaj and Ismail, 2017; Fadi and Maria, 2018; Ma and Yamaji, 2006). Meanwhile, lignin as the second major biopolymer component of cell walls can maintain the cell wall rigidity by cross-linking cellulose and hemicellulose, improving the resistance of cell walls and preventing microbial degradation (Yoo et al., 2020). In this study, the lignin contents of rice exposed to SiO2 ENMs (5 mg‧L−1) and F-SiO2 ENMs (5 mg‧L−1) were significantly higher (32.82% and 30.13%) than those of other treatments (Fig. S9). The fluorescence microscope images and the heat maps of sectioned stems (Fig. 4e) further confirmed the increase of lignin content. Moreover, the heat maps indicated that the blue fluorescence of lignin reached maximum at the edge of the stems from F-SiO2 ENMs (5 mg‧L−1) treatment, demonstrating the high lignin content in the outer layer of the rice stem upon F-SiO2 ENMs (5 mg‧L−1) exposure and a possible enhanced resistance of rice stems. The enhanced pest resistance of rice could alleviate the negative effect of planthopper on plant growth, an increased biomass was even observed in our study as shown in Fig. 4b and c.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular mechanisms of rice resistance to planthopper enhanced by SiO2 ENMs
ce:section.ce:para: The transcriptome data of rice stem further revealed the mechanisms of enhanced rice resistance to planthopper by SiO2 ENMs at a molecular level. In general, 349 genes (171 upregulation, 178 downregulation) were changed after F-SiO2 ENMs (5 mg‧L−1) exposure (Fig. 5a and b). It known that planthopper is a typical phloem-sucking herbivore and is one of the most serious and destructive insect pests in rice-growing areas (Liu et al., 2015). Moreover, it can serve as a vector to transmit rice grassy stunt virus and ragged stunt virus (Du et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2015). Plant hormones play vital roles in plant growth and development, as well as the regulation of plant resistance (Pan et al., 2018). Particularly, JA, SA, and ET play important roles in enhancing plant resistance to planthopper (Ye et al., 2013). However, in this study, the gene expressions of JA (A2Y806) and SA (B8AA73, A2WZ25, NPR1, A2XKE1, and A2ZBA9) remained unchanged after exposure to F-SiO2 ENMs (5 mg‧L−1) (Fig. S12). On the other hand, the auxin (IAA) signal transduction-related genes (A2WUX4 and GH3, q < 0.05) were upregulated by 1.66- and 2.36-fold (Fig. 5c). Studies have demonstrated that IAA can promote the enlargement of plant cells and increase plant growth (Barbez et al., 2017; Du et al., 2020). The upregulated IAA-related genes supported the evidence of increased biomass after planthopper infestation with pre-treatment of F-SiO2 ENMs (5 mg‧L−1) (Fig. 4b and c). Besides, the F-SiO2 ENMs (5 mg‧L−1) exposure led to the up-regulation of genes related to the reinforcement of cell wall (A2WZ30, 2.73-fold, q < 0.05), the induction of programmed cell death (B8BCT6, 1.35-fold, q < 0.05) and the activation of ascorbic acid glutathione metabolic pathway (A2YX67, 1.49-fold, q < 0.05) (Fig. 5c). Together with the results of increased lignin formation, F-SiO2 ENMs (5 mg‧L−1) can not only strengthen the physical defense of rice stem but also improve the ability to eliminate reactive oxygen species (ROS) induced by rice planthopper invasion. Meanwhile, a long chain wax related gene (B8AP99) was upregulated by 1.89-fold (q < 0.05) after F-SiO2 ENMs (5 mg‧L−1) exposure (Fig. 5c), which is very important for plant resistance to pests and diseases, and for prevention of non-stomatal loss of water in leaves (Oliveira et al., 2020). In the metabolism pathways of glucose and carbon fixation, the expression of ribulose 6-phosphate, sucrose and glucose synthesis related genes (A2XRR2, B8BHM9, B8BF84, B8A9F5, B8BF02, B8AZZ8, and A2YNH4) were upregulated by 1.71, 1.91, 2.71, 1.68, 1.97, 1.83, and 2.13-fold, respectively (Fig. 5c, q < 0.05). These substances contribute to the synthesis of fructose 6-phosphate, which has an indirect effect on increasing the synthesis of lignin, and a direct effect on improving the growth of rice seedlings. The upregulation of these genes supports our finding of the increased lignin content after F-SiO2 ENMs exposure (Fig. 4d). Therefore, the transcriptome data revealed that F-SiO2 ENMs enhanced the growth and resistance of rice seedlings from the following aspects: 1) enhanced carbon fixation and metabolism, which increased the lignin synthesis; 2) promoted auxin transduction for plant growth; 3) reinforced cell wall and triggered programmed cell necrosis, strengthening the physical defense of rice stems; 4) enhanced ROS scavenging capacity (Fig. 5d).

In this study, we demonstrated the mechanisms of SiO2 ENMs in enhancing the rice resistance to planthopper. With the help of fluorescence technology, the uptake and translocation of SiO2 ENMs in rice from root to shoot were observed. SiO2 ENMs can induce the resistance to planthopper, especially via physical defense (the enhanced lignin synthesis, reinforced cell wall, formation of silica cell analogs, and increased long chain wax). Besides, the expression of pest-related genes was upregulated in the presence of F-SiO2 ENMs (5 mg‧L−1). Thus, this study provides strong evidence for the environmentally friendly SiO2 ENMs, as newly developed SiO2-based nanopesticides for the sustainable agriculture practices.

Bingxu Cheng: Investigation, Writing-original draft, Writing-review & editing. Feiran Chen: Writing-review & editing, Formal analysis. Chuanxi Wang: Writing-review & editing, Formal analysis. Xiaofei Liu: Investigation, Material synthesis. Le Yue: Investigation, Data analysis. Xuesong Cao: Investigation, Data analysis. Zhenyu Wang: Experimental design, Investigation, Data analysis, Writing-review & editing. Baoshan Xing: Writing-review & editing.
